flashcards.front: What does LR stand for in LR parsing?
flashcards.back: L = Left-to-right scanning of input. R = Rightmost derivation in reverse (bottom-up). LR parsers read input from left to right and construct a rightmost derivation by working backwards from the input.

flashcards.front: What are the three main components of an LR parser?
flashcards.back:
1. Input buffer (holds the string to be parsed with $ marker)
2. Stack (stores alternating states and grammar symbols)
3. Parsing table (ACTION table and GOTO table)

flashcards.front: What are the four possible actions in the ACTION table of an LR parser?
flashcards.back:
1. Shift s: Push input symbol and state s onto stack
2. Reduce A->beta: Pop |beta| items, push A and GOTO state
3. Accept: Parsing successful
4. Error: Syntax error detected

An LR(0) item is a production rule with a dot (.) at some position in the right-hand side. The dot indicates how much of the production has been seen so far. For example, A -> X.YZ means X has been matched, and Y and Z are expected.

flashcards.front: What is the CLOSURE operation in LR parsing?
flashcards.back: CLOSURE(I) computes the complete set of items for a state. If item [A -> alpha.B beta] is in I, then for every production B -> gamma, add [B -> .gamma] to I. Repeat until no new items can be added.

flashcards.front: What is the GOTO function in LR parsing?
flashcards.back: GOTO(I, X) computes the set of items reachable from item set I on grammar symbol X. It finds all items [A -> alpha.X beta] in I, moves the dot past X to get [A -> alphaX.beta], and returns the CLOSURE of this new set.

A shift-reduce conflict occurs when the parser cannot decide whether to shift the next input symbol onto the stack or to reduce the symbols already on the stack using a production rule. It indicates the grammar is ambiguous for that parser type.

A reduce-reduce conflict occurs when the parser has two or more possible reductions it could apply at the same point, meaning multiple complete items exist in the same state for the same lookahead symbol.

flashcards.front: How do SLR, LALR, and CLR parsers differ?
flashcards.back: SLR uses FOLLOW sets to resolve conflicts (simple, small tables). LALR merges states with the same core (used by YACC, moderate power). CLR uses explicit lookahead in items (most powerful but largest tables).

flashcards.front: What does the GOTO table specify in an LR parser?
flashcards.back: GOTO[s, A] specifies the next state to transition to when non-terminal A has been recognized (after a reduction) and the current state on top of the stack is s.
